questions.text: I find that the felt weight of others' suffering is the moral experience that most clearly tells me which of my many possible actions would be most worth taking.
I feel that the moral responsibility generated by knowledge of suffering is not something I can opt out of without cost to my integrity.
I find that stressful situations don't noticeably change my heart rate or breathing.
I experience relief as a full-body release, almost like physical collapse.
I believe that building sustainable systems is more important than achieving quick wins.
I focus on what is being said rather than what is being implied.
I find that I register the emotional weight of a pause in conversation.
I prefer to pitch my idea and get others on board with it.
I tend to choose careers and projects based on how much they allow me to help others.
I am quick to take the microphone, literally or figuratively.
I find that my emotional responses sometimes feel disproportionate, yet entirely genuine.
I routinely make lifestyle choices that reflect my personal standards and aspirations.
I maintain a clean inbox and respond to messages within a consistent timeframe.
I find it natural to defer my own interests when group needs arise.
I find it energizing to be part of a team that navigates challenges together.
I detect the social undercurrents at a family gathering that newcomers would miss entirely.
I eat when I'm hungry and exercise when I feel like it, without a fixed schedule.
I think that my analytical approach to human pain allows me to contribute in ways that emotionally driven approaches cannot.
